title: Congenital CMV Infection
chief_complaint: Newborn with petechial rash, small head size, and failed newborn hearing screen. Born at term but SGA (small for gestational age). Mother had 'flu-like illness' during pregnancy.

physical_exam.Skin: Diffuse petechiae (thrombocytopenia). Blueberry muffin rash (extramedullary hematopoiesis).
physical_exam.Neurologic: Hypotonia. Poor suck. Failed ABR hearing screen bilaterally (sensorineural hearing loss).
physical_exam.Head: Anterior fontanelle full but soft.

hidden_data.urine_cmv: Urine CMV PCR: POSITIVE (within first 3 weeks of life - confirms congenital not perinatal infection)
hidden_data.blood_cmv: CMV viral load: 50,000 copies/mL (high viral load)
hidden_data.cmp: ALT 180, AST 150, Direct Bilirubin 3.5, Total Bilirubin 8.0
hidden_data.head_us: Intracranial calcifications (periventricular). Ventriculomegaly. No active hydrocephalus.
hidden_data.audiology: Bilateral severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss on ABR
hidden_data.ophthalmology: Chorioretinitis OU. No optic atrophy currently.
hidden_data.maternal_serology: Maternal CMV IgG and IgM positive during pregnancy (seroconversion - primary infection in 2nd trimester)

correct_diagnosis: Symptomatic Congenital CMV Infection (Primary Maternal Infection) with Microcephaly, Intracranial Calcifications, SNHL, Chorioretinitis, Hepatitis, and Thrombocytopenia
key_learning_points: CMV = most common congenital infection (0.5-2% of live births). Leading infectious cause of sensorineural hearing loss and neurodevelopmental delay
Primary maternal infection during pregnancy: 30-40% transmission rate. Reactivation/reinfection: 1-2% transmission. Risk highest in first trimester (more severe disease)

Symptomatic at birth (10-15%): Petechiae + thrombocytopenia (70%), IUGR, Microcephaly, Intracranial calcifications (periventricular - classic), Chorioretinitis, SNHL (35% - may be progressive, delayed onset), Hepatosplenomegaly, Hepatitis
Diagnosis: CMV PCR of urine/saliva within first 3 weeks of life (differentiates congenital from perinatal - which is usually asymptomatic). After 3 weeks cannot distinguish from postnatal acquisition

Treatment: Antiviral therapy (Valganciclovir 16mg/kg/dose PO BID or Ganciclovir IV) for 6 months for symptomatic congenital CMV with CNS involvement. Improves hearing and neurodevelopmental outcomes at 2 years. Side effects: Neutropenia (monitor ANC weekly). NOT recommended for asymptomatic or mild disease

Prognosis: Symptomatic: 50-90% develop sequelae (SNHL, CP, intellectual disability, vision loss). Asymptomatic: 10-15% develop SNHL (usually delayed onset). ALL children with congenital CMV need serial audiology and ophthalmology regardless of symptoms
